questions.question: Why is explicit requirement prioritization important before deep architecture discussion?
options: Because it helps the candidate choose what to optimize first and what trade-offs are acceptable.
Because interviewers require priorities to be numbered in every answer format.
Because it removes the need for scope cuts.
Because it guarantees the cheapest design is also the most reliable.
questions.explanation: Priority order makes architecture choices coherent instead of trying to maximize everything.

questions.question: Which pair of goals is most likely to be in tension for a global user-facing system?
options: Latency and cost.
Documentation and indentation style.
README length and code comments.
Directory naming and unit tests.
questions.explanation: Aggressive latency optimization often increases infrastructure cost.

questions.question: What is the clearest danger of declaring too many priorities as equally top-tier?
options: The interviewer will assume you are optimizing for only one user persona.
The design loses a clear decision rule, so trade-offs become vague and internally inconsistent.
The code artifact becomes impossible to run.
It prevents any capacity estimation later.
questions.explanation: Flat priorities reduce architecture to wishful thinking.

questions.question: Which architecture choice most directly reflects prioritizing availability over strict consistency?
options: Accepting some asynchronous replication or bounded staleness so the system remains available under partition or failure.
Removing all replication to simplify writes.
Avoiding caches because they are hard to explain.
Using a larger README file.
questions.explanation: Availability-first systems often accept weaker consistency to preserve service continuity.

questions.question: Why is it useful to confirm at least one requirement you will not optimize first?
options: It demonstrates realism by showing that some goals are deliberately second-order rather than accidentally ignored.
It lets you skip the trade-off section later.
It proves the omitted requirement is unimportant forever.
It guarantees no redesign follow-up will be asked.
questions.explanation: Stating what is second-order makes the optimization boundary explicit.

questions.question: What metric best indicates healthy prioritization discipline?
options: The number of different technologies named in the architecture.
How many major design choices are justified by the ranked requirement order.
How many bullet points appear in the requirements section.
The number of metrics dashboards planned.
questions.explanation: Priorities matter only if they guide actual design decisions.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with limiting yourself to a top-three priority list?
options: It keeps the answer sharp and actionable, but some nuance must be deferred to later discussion.
It prevents any mention of compliance or security.
It always produces under-designed systems.
It makes follow-up questions impossible to answer.
questions.explanation: A short list improves clarity even though it is not exhaustive.

questions.question: If the interviewer suddenly says compliance now outranks latency, what should you do first?
options: Continue the original design and mention compliance only at the end.
Restate the priority order, then explain which architecture decisions change under the new ranking.
Remove all caches without explanation.
Start over from the problem statement without summarizing the delta.
questions.explanation: Reprioritization should be explicit so the redesign feels controlled rather than chaotic.
